intro.why: Lessons 1 through 3 built an increasingly sophisticated toolkit for estimating VaR — parametric, non-parametric, and extreme-value approaches. But a model is only as good as its track record: a VaR estimate that looks elegant on paper is worthless if it consistently understates how often losses actually breach it. Backtesting is the discipline that closes this loop, turning ‘what does the model predict’ into ‘was the model right, and how do we know’. This lesson builds the statistical toolkit for answering that question, then shows how Basel turned it into a regulatory framework with real capital consequences.
intro.content:
The learning outcomes move from the basic mechanics of backtesting to an increasingly rigorous set of statistical tests, then to the regulatory framework built on top of them:

  1. Backtesting Fundamentals — Exceptions — defines backtesting and exceptions, and explains why the exception rate is the quantity to watch.
  2. Why Backtesting Is Hard — the static-portfolio assumption and non-market-risk items in actual P&L complicate a seemingly simple comparison.
  3. Failure Rates and the Z-Score Test — the simplest formal test: is the observed exception rate close enough to what the model promised?
  4. Type I/II Errors and the Kupiec Test — frames backtesting as a hypothesis test with two failure modes, and introduces a rigorous likelihood-ratio test for the overall exception rate.
  5. Conditional Coverage Testing — adds a timing dimension: are exceptions independent over time, or do they cluster?
  6. Basel Rules for Backtesting — turns all of the above into the green/yellow/red zone system that determines a bank's capital add-on.

Throughline: a VaR number is a promise about how often losses of a given size should occur — backtesting is how that promise gets checked, and the Basel zones are what happens when it's broken.

los.lo: LO 4.a — Define backtesting and exceptions, and explain the importance of backtesting VaR models
los.title: Backtesting Fundamentals: Exceptions and Why They Matter
los.why: Every VaR model is, at its core, a falsifiable prediction: ‘losses worse than X should happen only (1−c)% of the time.’ Without a systematic way to check that prediction against what actually happened, a VaR model is just an assumption dressed up as a number. This LO introduces the vocabulary — exceptions, failure rate — that every statistical test in the rest of this lesson is built on.
los.keyIdea: Backtesting compares a VaR model's predictions to actual P&L day by day; a day where the loss exceeds VaR is an exception, and the rate of exceptions should match the model's stated confidence level.
los.content:
What backtesting is

Backtesting compares a VaR model's predicted losses against a portfolio's actual profit and loss over a historical testing period. Each day, the actual loss is checked against that day's VaR estimate. If the actual loss is larger than the VaR prediction, that day is counted as an exception (also called an exceedance or a breach). If the actual loss stays within the VaR prediction, the model passed that day.

Why exceptions matter

A VaR figure is, by construction, a threshold that should be breached only a known fraction of the time. A 95% confidence, one-day VaR says: on a typical day, there is only a 5% chance that tomorrow's loss exceeds this number. Over a long enough testing window, the fraction of days that turn out to be exceptions should be close to that 5%.

  • Too many exceptions → the model is understating risk (VaR is too low).
  • Far fewer exceptions than expected → the model may be overstating risk (VaR is too conservative) — wasteful from a capital-efficiency standpoint, even though it isn't dangerous.

Backtesting is therefore the primary tool for answering can we trust this model — both for a bank's own risk managers and for regulators. Under the Basel framework (LO 4.f), a bank that racks up more than four exceptions in a 250-day testing window faces capital penalties.
takeaways: Backtesting compares predicted VaR against actual P&L, day by day.
An exception is a day where the actual loss exceeds the VaR prediction.
Too many exceptions means the model understates risk; far too few means it's overly conservative (costly but not dangerous).
The exception rate is the thread that runs through every test in this lesson, up to and including the Basel zones (LO 4.f).

los.lo: LO 4.b — Explain the significant difficulties in backtesting VaR models, including the use of actual versus hypothetical (cleaned) returns
los.title: Why Backtesting Is Hard: Static Portfolios and Cleaned Returns
los.why: A naive backtest — just comparing yesterday's VaR to today's reported P&L — is comparing two things that don't quite match: a prediction made for a frozen portfolio against an outcome generated by a portfolio that kept trading, plus P&L items the VaR model never tried to capture. This LO explains those mismatches and the practical fixes, which matter because every test in LOs 4.c–4.e assumes the exception count it's fed is a fair comparison in the first place.
los.keyIdea: VaR assumes a static portfolio and pure market-risk P&L, but real portfolios change intraday and real P&L includes fees, commissions, and trading gains — so backtesting requires care about holding period and which P&L series to test.
los.content:
The static-portfolio problem

A VaR estimate is calculated for the portfolio as it stands today, holding positions fixed over the VaR horizon. In reality, a trading desk's portfolio changes constantly — positions are added, closed, and resized throughout the day. Comparing yesterday's VaR to today's actual P&L therefore compares a static-portfolio prediction against a P&L that reflects a portfolio that didn't stay static.

Actual P&L contains more than market risk

The VaR model is built to capture market risk — changes in prices, rates, and volatilities. But the actual daily P&L a bank reports also includes items the VaR model never tried to predict: trading commissions and fees, net interest income earned on positions, bid-ask spread effects from market making, and gains or losses from intraday trades opened and closed before the end of the day. These items can push actual P&L away from what the VaR model alone would predict, even on a day when market-risk factors behaved exactly as expected.

The fix: shorter horizons and cleaned returns

The standard practical response is to backtest using a one-day holding period — shrinking the window between the VaR estimate and the P&L comparison limits how much the portfolio (and the other P&L-distorting items) can drift in the meantime. A complementary fix distinguishes two P&L series: actual returns (everything the bank reported, including the non-market-risk items above) versus cleaned (hypothetical) returns (P&L recalculated to strip out fees, commissions, and intraday trading effects, leaving only what the static end-of-day portfolio earned or lost from market moves).

Best practice is to backtest both series. If a model passes on cleaned returns but fails on actual returns, that points to a problem with how non-market-risk P&L items are handled — not with the VaR model's core risk-factor assumptions.

Choosing a holding period

More generally, the VaR holding period reflects two considerations: how long it would actually take to liquidate or hedge the position, and how long the portfolio can reasonably be assumed not to change for reasons unrelated to market risk. In practice, banks most commonly use a one-day holding period, which is also the natural choice for daily backtesting.
takeaways: VaR assumes the portfolio is frozen over the horizon; real portfolios change continuously.
Actual P&L includes fees, commissions, net interest income, and intraday trading gains/losses that VaR never tried to predict.
Cleaned (hypothetical) returns strip those items out, leaving only mark-to-market moves on the static portfolio.
Best practice: backtest both series — passing on cleaned but failing on actual points to non-market-risk P&L, not the model itself.
A one-day holding period is standard, balancing liquidation time against how long the portfolio realistically stays static.

los.lo: LO 4.c — Verify a model based on exceptions, or failure rates
los.title: Failure Rates and the Z-Score Test
los.why: This is the first concrete, computable test in the lesson — a direct, intuitive way to ask whether the exception rate from LO 4.a is statistically close enough to what the model promised. It's also the foundation the more rigorous Kupiec test (LO 4.d) generalizes.
los.keyIdea: The z-score test turns the observed failure rate N/T into a yes/no verdict on whether the model's exception rate matches its stated confidence level.
los.content:
The failure rate

Define T as the number of days (observations) in the testing sample, N as the number of exceptions observed, and p = 1 − c as the probability of an exception implied by the model's confidence level c. The failure rate is simply N / T — the observed proportion of days on which the model was breached. If the model is correctly specified, N/T should converge to p as T grows large. A failure rate persistently and substantially different from p is evidence the model's confidence level is mis-calibrated.

The z-score test

Under the null hypothesis that the model is correctly calibrated, N is approximately binomial with mean pT and variance p(1−p)T. For reasonably large T this is approximated by a normal distribution, giving the test statistic z = (N − pT) / √(p(1−p)T). Under the null, z is approximately standard normal; at 95% confidence the critical value is ±1.96, so |z| > 1.96 rejects the hypothesis that the model is unbiased.

Worked example. A bank's actual losses exceeded its one-day 95% VaR on 22 of the last 252 trading days. p = 0.05, T = 252, N = 22: pT = 12.6, and √(p(1−p)T) = √(0.05 × 0.95 × 252) ≈ 3.46. So z = (22 − 12.6) / 3.46 ≈ 2.72. Since 2.72 > 1.96, the null hypothesis is rejected: 22 exceptions is too many for a well-calibrated 95% VaR model over this sample, and the model likely understates risk.

Interpreting the test

The z-score test is a quick, intuitive screen, but it has the weakness of any test based purely on a count of exceptions: it says nothing about when they occurred. A model that produces exactly the expected number of exceptions, but has them all clustered in a two-week stretch of market stress, would pass this test while still having a serious flaw — the gap that conditional coverage testing (LO 4.e) addresses. The more formal, likelihood-based version of this same question is Kupiec's test (LO 4.d).
los.formula:
Failure rate = N / T, expected exceptions = p × T (p = 1 − c)
z = (N − pT) / √(p(1 − p)T)
Reject "unbiased" if |z| > 1.96 (95% critical value)
takeaways: Failure rate = N/T should converge to p = 1−c if the model is correctly calibrated.
z = (N − pT) / √(p(1−p)T); reject the unbiasedness hypothesis if |z| > 1.96.
Worked example: 22 exceptions in 252 days at 95% VaR → z ≈ 2.72 → reject (model understates risk).
The z-test only looks at the count of exceptions, not their timing — the gap conditional coverage testing fills (LO 4.e).

los.lo: LO 4.d — Define Type I and Type II errors, and describe Kupiec's test of unconditional coverage
los.title: Type I and Type II Errors and the Kupiec Test
los.why: Any backtest is a hypothesis test, and any hypothesis test can fail in two directions. This LO names those two failure modes explicitly, shows just how severe the tradeoff is at Basel's 99%/250-day setup, and then gives a more rigorous, likelihood-based version of the z-test from LO 4.c — the test that the Basel zones in LO 4.f are ultimately built on.
los.keyIdea: Any backtest can fail by rejecting a good model (Type I) or accepting a bad one (Type II); Kupiec's LR_uc test formalizes the unconditional-coverage question — is the overall exception rate right — as a likelihood-ratio test rather than a fixed cutoff.
los.content:
Two ways a backtest can go wrong

Type I error: rejecting a VaR model that is actually correctly calibrated, based on a sample that happens to show too many exceptions purely by chance. Type II error: failing to reject (i.e., accepting) a VaR model that is actually mis-calibrated, because the sample happened to show too few exceptions for that window. A good backtesting framework tries to keep both error rates low, but there's an inherent tension — a wider acceptance band reduces Type I errors but increases Type II errors, and vice versa.

The binomial framework, and why 99%/250 days is hard

Under the null, N follows a binomial distribution with parameters T and p. For Basel's 99% confidence / 250-day setup, p = 0.01 and the expected number of exceptions is 250 × 0.01 = 2.5. Basel treats 5 or more exceptions as the threshold for scrutiny (LO 4.f). How often would a correctly calibrated 99% model produce 5 or more exceptions just by chance? About 10.8% — the Type I error rate of a “reject if N ≥ 5” rule. Now flip it: how often would a model whose true confidence is only 97% (i.e., meaningfully too aggressive) produce fewer than 5 exceptions and escape scrutiny? About 12.8% — the Type II error rate. With only 250 daily observations and an expected count of just 2.5, a simple exception-count rule at 99% confidence cannot make both error rates small at once.

Kupiec's likelihood ratio test for unconditional coverage

Rather than a fixed cutoff, Kupiec proposed a likelihood ratio test comparing the likelihood of the observed data under the model's stated probability p versus under the sample's own observed failure rate N/T. This is a test of unconditional coverage — it asks only whether the overall proportion of exceptions is right, without regard to timing (timing is LO 4.e). LR_uc is asymptotically chi-squared distributed with 1 degree of freedom; at 95% confidence the critical value is 3.84 (= 1.96², the square of the z-test's critical value). Reject the null if LR_uc > 3.84.

Worked example. A bank backtests its one-day 95% VaR (p = 0.05) over T = 252 days and observes N = 12 exceptions (close to the expected pT = 12.6). Evaluating the likelihood ratio gives LR_uc ≈ 0.03, far below 3.84 — the null is not rejected: 12 exceptions out of 252 is entirely consistent with a well-calibrated 95% VaR model.

Nonrejection regions narrow as confidence rises

A useful way to visualize the Kupiec test is as a nonrejection region for N, for a given T and p. At 97.5% confidence (p = 0.025) with T = 252, the nonrejection region for N is roughly [2, 12]. With a larger sample, T = 1,000, it's roughly [15, 36] — narrower in percentage terms. At 95% confidence (p = 0.05) with T = 252, it's roughly [6, 20]. The general pattern: nonrejection bands narrow as T grows, and narrow in absolute exception-count terms as the required confidence level rises (smaller p) — precisely why backtesting a 99% VaR model is statistically harder than backtesting a 95% model with the same sample size.
los.formula:
LR_uc = −2 ln[ (1−p)^(T−N) p^N / (1 − N/T)^(T−N) (N/T)^N ]
LR_uc ~ chi-squared(1); critical value at 95% = 3.84 (= 1.96²)
Reject unconditional coverage if LR_uc > 3.84
takeaways: Type I error = rejecting a correctly calibrated model (false alarm); Type II error = failing to reject a mis-calibrated model.
At Basel's 99%/250-day setup, a correct model triggers the “N ≥ 5” flag ~10.8% of the time (Type I), while a truly-97% model escapes it ~12.8% of the time (Type II).
LR_uc = −2 ln[likelihood ratio] ~ chi-squared(1); reject if LR_uc > 3.84 (same threshold as z² > 1.96²).
Worked example: p=0.05, T=252, N=12 → LR_uc ≈ 0.03, far below 3.84 → not rejected.
Nonrejection bands narrow as T grows and as required confidence rises — why 99% VaR is statistically harder to backtest than 95% VaR.

los.lo: LO 4.e — Describe the framework of conditional and unconditional coverage, and explain Christoffersen's test of independence
los.title: Conditional Coverage Testing
los.why: The Kupiec test (LO 4.d) only asks did we get roughly the right number of exceptions — it can't tell a healthy, spread-out exception pattern from a dangerous cluster. This LO closes that gap, and the combined test it introduces (LR_cc) is the more complete unconditional-plus-timing check that sits just below the Basel zones in LO 4.f.
los.keyIdea: Conditional coverage testing adds a timing dimension Kupiec's test misses: it checks whether exceptions cluster together (LR_ind), then combines that with the unconditional coverage test (LR_uc) into one joint test (LR_cc).
los.content:
The blind spot in unconditional coverage

LR_uc treats the T daily pass/fail outcomes as if only their total count N matters. Two very different sequences — 12 exceptions spread roughly evenly across a 252-day year, versus the same 12 exceptions all occurring within a single two-week stretch — have the identical failure rate N/T = 12/252, so both produce the same LR_uc. But the second pattern, a cluster of exceptions, is a red flag in its own right: it suggests the model failed to adapt when market conditions changed (e.g., correlations or volatilities shifted and the model kept using stale estimates), even if it averages out to a reasonable count over the full year.

Christoffersen's test of independence (LR_ind)

LR_ind examines whether exceptions are serially independent — whether an exception on one day makes an exception on the next day more or less likely than the model implies. Like LR_uc, LR_ind is asymptotically chi-squared distributed with 1 degree of freedom, so the same critical value applies: reject the hypothesis of independence if LR_ind > 3.84. A significant LR_ind — exceptions clustering more than chance would predict — indicates the model isn't adapting quickly enough to changing conditions, even if its long-run exception rate looks fine.

Combining the two: conditional coverage (LR_cc)

Conditional coverage combines both questions — is the overall rate right, and are exceptions independent over time — into LR_cc = LR_uc + LR_ind. Because each component is asymptotically chi-squared with 1 degree of freedom and (under the joint null) independent of the other, their sum is asymptotically chi-squared with 2 degrees of freedom; at 95% confidence the critical value is 5.99. Reject the model under conditional coverage if LR_cc > 5.99. A model can fail conditional coverage even if it passes unconditional coverage on its own — for example, if the exception count is about right (LR_uc small) but exceptions are heavily clustered (LR_ind large enough to push LR_cc past 5.99).

Why this matters in practice

If a model's exceptions are serially dependent, the appropriate response isn't just to flag the model as wrong in some generic sense — it's a specific diagnostic that the model's risk-factor correlations, volatilities, or other inputs likely need to be re-estimated to reflect current market conditions, since the clustering suggests the model lagged a real shift in the environment.
los.formula:
LR_ind ~ chi-squared(1); reject independence if LR_ind > 3.84
LR_cc = LR_uc + LR_ind ~ chi-squared(2)
Reject conditional coverage if LR_cc > 5.99
takeaways: LR_uc alone can't distinguish 12 evenly-spread exceptions from 12 exceptions clustered in two weeks — both give the same LR_uc.
Clustering is itself a red flag: it suggests the model didn't adapt when correlations or volatilities shifted.
LR_ind tests independence (chi-squared(1), critical value 3.84); LR_cc = LR_uc + LR_ind tests both together (chi-squared(2), critical value 5.99).
A model can pass unconditional coverage (right overall rate) but fail conditional coverage (clustered exceptions).

los.lo: LO 4.f — Explain the Basel rules for backtesting models, including the green, yellow, and red zones, and discuss the tradeoffs in the backtesting confidence level
los.title: Basel Rules for Backtesting
los.why: This is where every statistical idea in the lesson — exceptions, failure rates, Type I/II errors, Kupiec's test — lands as an actual regulatory consequence. The green/yellow/red framework is the concrete answer to so what happens when a model fails its backtest, and the 10.8%/12.8% error rates from LO 4.d explain why the framework is designed the way it is.
los.keyIdea: Basel turns the exception count from a 99%/250-day backtest into a green, yellow, or red zone, which directly scales the multiplier applied to a bank's market-risk capital charge.
los.content:
The setup: 99% confidence, 250 trading days

Basel requires banks using internal models for market-risk capital to compute a one-day VaR at the 99% confidence level and backtest it over the most recent 250 trading days (roughly one year). Under the null that the model is correctly calibrated, p = 1 − 0.99 = 0.01, so the expected number of exceptions is 250 × 0.01 = 2.5 per year.

The three zones

ZoneExceptions (N)Multiplier add-on (k)Outcome
Green0–4k = 3 (no increase)No penalty — consistent with a well-calibrated model
Yellow5–9k rises by 0.40–0.85, scaled to NSupervisor evaluates the cause and decides whether a penalty applies
Red≥10k automatically +1 (k = 4)Automatic penalty — model presumed inadequate

The multiplier k is applied to the model's VaR-based capital charge, so moving from green to yellow or red directly increases the capital a bank must hold against market risk.

The yellow zone: four possible explanations

Because 5–9 exceptions could plausibly arise even from a sound model (recall the 10.8% Type I error rate from LO 4.d), the yellow zone is not an automatic penalty. The supervisor investigates the cause and classifies it into one of four categories: (1) basic integrity of the model is lacking — implementation or data errors, penalty applies; (2) model accuracy needs improvement — risk-factor coverage or volatility/correlation estimates are inadequate, penalty applies; (3) intraday trading activity — losses came from positions opened and closed within the day, which the static end-of-day VaR model was never designed to capture, penalty considered with more latitude; (4) bad luck (market conditions) — volatile markets produced more large moves than usual, consistent with a correctly specified model simply drawing an unlucky sample — no penalty mandated.

The fundamental difficulty

As shown in LO 4.d, applying the “N ≥ 5” yellow-zone trigger to a genuinely accurate 99% model produces a 10.8% Type I error rate — about 1 in 10 sound models land in yellow or red purely by chance in a given year. Meanwhile, a model that is meaningfully too aggressive (true confidence ≈97%) still stays green about 12.8% of the time (Type II). With only 250 observations and an expected count of just 2.5, there isn't enough data to make both error rates small at 99% confidence.

An alternative: lower the required confidence level

One proposal is to require a lower VaR confidence level — say 95% instead of 99% — while compensating with a larger capital multiplier, so the capital outcome for a well-behaved model is similar. At 95% confidence over 250 days, expected exceptions rise to 250 × 0.05 = 12.5, giving the test much more power: using a threshold around 17+ exceptions, the Type I error rate is roughly 12.5% and the Type II error rate improves to roughly 7.4% (versus 12.8% at 99%/250 days).

Why backtesting confidence levels also matter for incentives

Moving from a 97% to a 99% confidence requirement for the same portfolio can require substantially more capital (one illustrative comparison puts the increase around 1.24×). Banks evaluated only on whether they clear a fixed exception-count bar therefore have an incentive to choose model assumptions that minimize measured VaR (and capital) while still clearing the bar — another reason regulators care about the statistical properties of the backtesting framework itself.
takeaways: Basel backtests 99% VaR over 250 days; expected exceptions = 2.5.
Green (0–4, k=3, no penalty), Yellow (5–9, k rises 0.40–0.85), Red (≥10, k+1=4, automatic penalty).
Yellow-zone causes: model integrity, model accuracy, intraday trading, or bad luck — only bad luck isn't penalized.
At 99%/250 days, even a correct model has ~10.8% chance of yellow/red (Type I); a truly-97% model stays green ~12.8% of the time (Type II).
A 95%/250-day alternative (expected ≈12.5 exceptions) gives more power: Type I ≈12.5%, Type II ≈7.4%.
